query_theme: data chart graph statistics science space nature human body history records surprising facts
topic_doctrine: Data Minute is a broad curiosity-driven DATA-EXPLAINER channel, NOT a finance/economics channel. Pick topics with a 'whoa, I didn't know that' hook: science, space, the human body, nature and animals, history, world records, mysteries, scale and superlatives. Avoid dry pocketbook/personal-finance topics (insurance premiums, rent, wages, taxes) unless there is a genuinely surprising, counterintuitive angle. Every video should make someone stop scrolling.

stories.title: Priced Out: How A House Quietly Doubled In Cost
stories.hook: One income bought your parents a house. Not anymore.
stories.closing: Same house, way more money. Welcome to the new rules.

segments.say: Your parents' house cost 270 thousand in 2019. Today, 449. Same house, 180 thousand more for nothing.
Then mortgage rates jumped from 3.9 percent to 6.6. Nearly double the interest, tens of thousands more for the same house.
So how long to buy? In San Jose, 11.3 years of your entire salary. It feels impossible because it is.

stories.question: Are you ever buying a house? Be honest below.

stories.title: Why Your Grocery Bill Keeps Winning
stories.hook: Same groceries. Way bigger receipt. Where'd it go?
stories.closing: Same cart, fatter receipt. Bon appétit.

segments.say: Since 2020, eggs are up 86 percent. Not 86 cents, 86 percent, while your paycheck barely moved.

segments.topic: the share of income spent on food
segments.say: Add it up and food now eats 11.8 percent of your income, the most in decades.
The average grocery bill hit 1,030 dollars a month, about 300 more than 2019. Same fridge.

stories.question: What's gotten insanely expensive at your store? Comment.

stories.title: Americans Owe $1.4 Trillion On Plastic
stories.hook: Americans owe more than ever. It's a trap.
stories.closing: Owe more, save less. The math always wins.

segments.say: Card debt just passed 1,370 billion dollars, nearly 1.4 trillion on plastic, climbing every month.
And interest hit 22.9 percent. At that rate your balance grows even if you never swipe again. That's the trap.
Meanwhile savings cratered, from 16.8 percent of income in 2020 to just 3.6. The cushion is gone.

stories.question: Credit card debt: winning or losing? Tell me below.

stories.title: Your Raise Was Fake (Here's The Math)
stories.hook: You got a raise. So why are you broker?
stories.closing: Bigger paycheck, smaller life. Cool.

segments.say: On paper you won. The average wage climbed to 31.9 dollars an hour. Your paycheck genuinely got bigger.
But adjust for prices and the wage index sits at 99, below 2019. After inflation, you earn less.

segments.topic: hours of work to cover rent
segments.say: Now it takes 69 hours of work to cover one month's rent, up from 52. Two extra days a month, gone.

stories.question: Did your raise actually help? Comment below.

stories.title: The Jobs Report Hides 3 Ugly Truths
stories.hook: Best job market ever? Then why's everyone broke?
stories.closing: Booming economy. For someone, somewhere.

segments.say: Unemployment sits at just 4.3 percent, historically low. That's the headline, but it hides a lot.
Right now 4.8 million people are stuck part-time who want full-time. Employed on paper, not by choice.

segments.topic: people working two or more jobs
segments.say: And 9 million now work two or more jobs, a record. Why does surviving take two paychecks?

stories.question: Is your job market good or brutal right now?

stories.title: Same Salary, Different State, Opposite Life
stories.hook: Same salary. One thrives, one drowns. Why?
stories.closing: Same salary, totally different planet.

segments.say: Cost of living swings wildly. At 184 on the index, Hawaii is about 84 percent pricier than average.
Incomes split too. Massachusetts tops near 96.5 thousand a household, almost double the poorest states.
And rent is the knockout. A one-bedroom in California averages 2,150 dollars, double the cheapest states.

stories.question: What state are you in, and is it survivable?

stories.title: You Pay More for Health Insurance and Get Less Every Year
stories.hook: Your plan didn't change. Your bill exploded. Why?
stories.closing: More money in, less protection. That's the deal.

segments.say: Your employer paid 8,120 dollars per worker for coverage in 2026, up from 6,015 in 2019. 35 percent more for the same plan.
Your deductible, what you pay before insurance kicks in, hit 2,340 dollars, up from 1,655. You pay more to wait longer.
And the out-of-pocket cap hit 5,620 dollars before insurance covers everything, up from 3,970. The safety net got a hole.

stories.question: What's your deductible this year? Drop it below.

stories.title: The Degree That Tripled in Cost But Pays Half as Much
stories.hook: A degree costs triple. It pays half.
stories.closing: Most expensive ever. Worth less than before.

segments.say: Total student debt hit 1.74 trillion dollars, double what it was in 2010. A new class graduates owing more every May.
The average student loan payment is 352 dollars a month, more than most car payments, for a degree you already have.
And the payoff shrank. The wage premium for a bachelor's fell from 66 percent in 2014 to 54 today. Cost up, advantage down.

stories.question: Was your degree worth it? Yes or no below.

stories.title: Most Americans Are Behind on Retirement. Here's How Far.
stories.hook: You're way behind on retirement. Here's how far.
stories.closing: Save more or work forever. Those are basically the options.

segments.say: The median 50-something has 130 thousand saved. The target is around 360 thousand. That's a 230,000 dollar hole mid-career.
Social Security used to replace 41 percent of your income. By 2026 it's down to 31. The gap just got wider.
So people work longer. Average retirement age climbed from 60 in 2000 to 66 today. Six extra years because the math didn't work.

stories.question: Are you actually on track to retire? Be honest below.

stories.title: A New Car Costs What a House Used To. Here's the Receipt.
stories.hook: A new car now costs what a house used to.
stories.closing: It's not the avocado toast. It's the car payment.

segments.say: The average new car hit 50,400 dollars in 2026, up from 38,000 in 2019. Same truck, 12,400 inflated.
Auto loan rates peaked at 8.1 percent, the highest since 2007. On a 50 thousand dollar car that's about 10,700 in interest.
Add it all up. Owning a car now runs 1,920 dollars a month, what a 2-bedroom rented for not long ago.

stories.question: What's your car payment? Drop the number below.

stories.title: We've Found 5,600 Worlds Beyond Our Own
stories.hook: We've found thousands of worlds beyond our own.
stories.closing: Turns out we're not the only address out here.

segments.say: Thirty years ago we knew of zero. Today, 5,600 confirmed worlds, almost all found since 2009.
But almost none are like home. Only about 210 are small rocky worlds like Earth.
And the closest, Proxima b, sits 4.2 light years away. That's 75,000 years at today's fastest spacecraft.

stories.question: Do you think we're alone out there? Comment below.

stories.title: You're Scared of the Wrong Things. Here's What Kills You.
stories.hook: You're scared of the wrong things. Here's proof.
stories.closing: Sharks are fine. Watch out for the couch.

segments.say: Here's what actually kills Americans. Heart disease takes 695 thousand a year, cancer 605 thousand. Nearly half of all deaths.

segments.topic: annual US deaths from things we fear
segments.say: Now what terrifies us. Sharks kill about 1 American a year, lightning 20. Your nightmares are a rounding error.
The real killers are habits. Smoking still takes 480 thousand a year, bad diet around 300 thousand. Slow daily choices.

stories.question: What are you irrationally scared of? Tell me below.

stories.title: Earth Lost Most of Its Wildlife in 50 Years
stories.hook: Earth lost most of its wildlife in one lifetime.
stories.closing: The world went quiet and nobody noticed.

segments.say: Since 1970, average wildlife populations shrank nearly three-quarters. The index fell from 100 to 27. Two of every three wild animals, gone.
It's worst in the tropics. Latin America lost 95 percent of its wildlife, Africa 76. The richest ecosystems empty fastest.
Freshwater life is vanishing fastest, down 85 percent. The rivers you grew up near hold a fraction of what they did.

stories.question: What animal do you never see anymore? Comment below.

stories.title: You Paid for Inflation Twice — Once at the Store, Once at the Bank
stories.hook: They hiked rates to fix prices. It fixed nothing.
stories.closing: The bill came due. You just didn't know it was yours.

segments.say: Eggs up 44 percent since 2020. Gas up 33, rent up 29, groceries up 25. That's a new price floor.
So the Fed hiked, from 0.08 percent in 2021 to 5.33 in 2023. The most aggressive cycle since the 1980s.
Did it work? Gas is still up 28.4 percent year over year. The stuff you can't avoid is still the most expensive.

stories.question: Are higher rates helping or hurting you? Comment.

stories.title: America Got 3 Times Fatter in 30 Years. Here's the Bill.
stories.hook: In 1990, 1 in 8 Americans was obese. Now it's almost 1 in 2.
stories.closing: Bigger country, bigger problem, bigger bill.

segments.say: In 1990, 12 percent of American adults were obese. By 2022 it's 41.9. A generation getting steadily heavier.
And 41.9 percent isn't just high. Japan is at 4.3, France 17. We are the global outlier.

segments.topic: annual US medical cost of obesity
segments.say: The medical bill? 215 billion dollars a year, up from 61 billion in 2000. More than most countries' entire defense budget.

stories.question: What changed since the nineties? Your theory below.

stories.title: A Third of America Is Running on Too Little Sleep. Here's What It Costs.
stories.hook: You're awake 16 hours. Your body needed 8 of them to recover.
stories.closing: Half a trillion dollars of tired. And counting.

segments.say: In 1985, 22 percent of adults slept under 7 hours. By 2024 it's 37. More than 1 in 3 are chronically under-slept.

segments.topic: increased health risk from chronic sleep deprivation
segments.say: Under 7 hours raises obesity risk 55 percent, heart disease 48, depression 55. You're breaking down while you scroll.

segments.topic: annual economic cost of sleep deprivation
segments.say: RAND put a number on it. 527 billion dollars a year in lost productivity and health. Tired is expensive.

stories.question: How many hours do you actually sleep? Drop it below.

stories.title: Nobody's Getting Married Anymore. Here's Where It Went.
stories.hook: Your grandparents married at 22. You might not marry at all.
stories.closing: Marriage didn't die. It just got priced out.

segments.say: In 1980, men married at 24.7. Today it's 30.5; women went from 22 to 28.6. A full decade shifted.
And fewer marry at all. The rate fell from 10.6 per thousand in 1980 to 5.8 today, nearly cut in half.

segments.topic: share of adults married by income quartile
segments.say: The top income quartile still marries at 58 percent. The bottom, 31. Marriage became a luxury stability buys.

stories.question: Married, single, or never? Drop it in the comments.

stories.title: You're Paying for Way More Subscriptions Than You Think
stories.hook: Subscriptions are draining you on purpose. Here's how.
stories.closing: Death by a thousand free trials.
stories.question: How many subscriptions are you actually paying for? Comment.

segments.topic: what people think vs really pay
segments.say: Each charge is tiny, so you ignore it. You feel like you spend 86 dollars a month. You actually spend 219.
They pile up because nothing stops one. The average home went from 3 subscriptions to 12 in six years.
And they know it. 48 percent pay for something they never use; 42 percent forgot they signed up. The forgetting is the model.

stories.caption: You think you spend about $86 a month on subscriptions. The real average is $219 — and it climbs every single year. Here's exactly where the money leaks, in 4 charts. How many subscriptions are you actually paying for? 👇

stories.title: We've Mapped Mars Better Than Our Own Ocean
stories.hook: Why do we know Mars better than our own ocean?
stories.closing: The final frontier is under the boat.
stories.question: Would you ride to the bottom of the ocean? Comment below.

segments.say: Space is easy to look at; a telescope sees all of Mars. But you can't see through water, so only 25 percent of our seafloor is mapped.
It's absurdly deep and dark. The Titanic sits at 3,800 meters; the deepest trench hits 11,000, where pressure would crush a sub.
We've named about 240 thousand ocean species and think there are 2 million. Nine in ten are still down there, unseen.

stories.caption: We have better maps of Mars and the Moon than our own ocean floor — only about 25% of it is mapped, and 9 in 10 ocean species are still unknown to science. The real final frontier is right under the boat. Would you ride to the bottom? 👇

stories.title: The Loneliness Epidemic, in Four Charts
stories.hook: Why is everyone lonely when we're this connected?
stories.closing: Never more connected. Never more alone.
stories.question: How many close friends do you actually have? Comment.

segments.say: In 1990 only 3 percent of Americans had zero close friends. Today it's 12, quadrupled. More people have no one to call.
Why? We stopped hanging out. In-person time with friends fell from 6.5 hours a week to 2.5. We swapped it for screens.
And chronic loneliness raises your risk of dying early by 26 percent, about the same as obesity. Your body treats alone like an injury.

stories.caption: In 1990, 3% of Americans said they had no close friends. Today it's 12% — and chronic loneliness raises your risk of early death by 26%, about the same as obesity. We've never been more connected, or more alone. How many close friends do you actually have? 👇

stories.title: Your Attention Span Collapsed. Here's the Data.
stories.hook: Why can't you focus anymore? It's not your fault.
stories.closing: Anyway, you probably already swiped away.
stories.question: Did you make it to the end? Be honest below.

segments.say: In 2004 people focused on a screen for 150 seconds before switching. Now it's 47. Something trained your brain to bail.
That something is short video. Daily time went from 20 minutes to 95, each swipe hitting the same reward chemical as a slot machine.
And every interruption takes 23 minutes to recover from. A full hour of work leaves maybe 40 real minutes of focus.

stories.caption: In 2004 the average person focused on a screen for 150 seconds before switching. Today? 47 seconds. Meanwhile daily short-video time nearly 5x'd. Your attention span didn't break by accident. Did you make it to the end? Be honest 👇

stories.title: We're Launching 10x More Rockets. SpaceX Is Why.
stories.hook: More rockets launched last year than all of the '90s.
stories.closing: Good news: internet everywhere. Bad news: space is getting crowded.
stories.question: Would you use Starlink internet? Drop it below.
stories.caption: In 2000 the world launched 80 rockets. In 2025 it was 272 — and SpaceX ran half of them. 62 percent of active satellites are now internet relays. Meanwhile tracked debris jumped from 9,000 to 44,000 objects. Is space worth it? 👇

segments.topic: global orbital rocket launches per year
segments.say: The world launched about 80 rockets in 2000. In 2025 it was 272, more than tripled, half of that in the last 5 years.
That's SpaceX. It ran 134 of 2025's 272 launches, nearly half. One private company out-launched entire nations.

segments.topic: tracked objects in Earth orbit (debris + active)
segments.say: But debris piles up. Tracked objects in low orbit went from 9,000 in 2000 to 44,000 today. One collision could lock us out.

stories.title: Americans Are Having Fewer Kids Than Ever. Here's the Math.
stories.hook: The U.S. birth rate just hit a record low.
stories.closing: We built an economy you can't afford to start a family in.
stories.question: Do you want kids someday? What's your biggest hold-up? Drop it below.
stories.caption: The U.S. fertility rate just hit 1.62 — below the 2.1 replacement level. Raising a child costs $310,000 before college. The median mom is now nearly 28 at first birth. And the U.S. gives zero days of federally mandated paid leave. Want kids? Tell me. 👇

segments.topic: U.S. total fertility rate (births per woman)
segments.say: The U.S. fertility rate fell from 3.65 in 1960 to 1.62 today. Below 2.1, the population shrinks. 2024 set a record low.

segments.topic: cost to raise a child to age 18 (2025 dollars)
segments.say: Why? Raising a child to 18 cost 198 thousand in 1990, in today's money. Now it's 310 thousand, before college.
Other rich countries fight it. Hungary gives 180 weeks of paid leave. The U.S. mandates 0. We treat a new kid as a personal problem.

stories.title: Why Tipping Went From 15% to 22% in 10 Years
stories.hook: You didn't choose 20 percent. Your iPad did.
stories.closing: The house always wins. So does the tip screen.
stories.question: Do you tip at coffee shops? Be honest below.
stories.caption: The average U.S. tip was 16.5 percent in 2010. In 2025 it's 22.4 — and spreading to takeout, coffee, and hotel housekeeping. The turning point: Square and Toast replaced cash registers in 2012 and set the default buttons at 18/20/25 instead of 15/18/20. One UX decision cost Americans $54 billion extra a year. Worth it? 👇

segments.say: In 2010 the standard tip was about 16.5 percent. By 2025 it's 22.4, not because service improved, but because tablets replaced registers.

segments.topic: average tip: pre-tablet vs. post-tablet POS
segments.say: When Square and Toast took over, they preset the buttons at 18, 20, and 25 percent instead of 15, 18, 20. The floor moved.
Stack it up. Americans tipped 66 billion dollars in 2010; by 2025 it's 120 billion, a 54 billion increase we never voted on.

stories.title: AI Is Quietly Eating The Power Grid
stories.hook: Big Tech is draining your power grid.
stories.closing: The cloud has a smokestack.
stories.question: Would you give up AI to save power? Tell me below.
stories.caption: Data centers burned 200 terawatt-hours in 2018 — now it's 580, and one AI answer uses ~10x a Google search. Here's the hidden cost of the cloud 👇

segments.say: Data centers used 200 terawatt hours in 2018. By 2026 it's 580, and AI is the reason.
One AI answer burns about 2.9 watt hours, nearly ten times a Google search at 0.3.
So their slice of US electricity jumps from 2.5 percent toward 9 percent by 2030.

stories.title: Gas Cars Just Hit Their Peak
stories.hook: Gas cars are finished. Big Oil knows.
stories.closing: The gas tank is on its way out.
stories.question: Would your next car be electric? Drop it below.
stories.caption: Battery prices crashed from $780 to ~$100 per kWh, electric hit 24% of new cars, and there are now 300+ models. The tipping point is here 👇

segments.say: Battery costs crashed from 780 dollars per kilowatt hour to about 100.
So electric jumped to 24 percent of new cars sold, up from just 2 percent.
And buyers now pick from over 300 models, six times more than a decade ago.

stories.title: Flying Got Worse — The Data Proves It
stories.hook: Flying got worse — and it's not the weather.
stories.closing: More flyers, same old runways.
stories.question: What's your worst flight delay story? Tell me below.
stories.caption: Airlines now move 5.2 billion passengers a year, on-time arrivals fell to 74%, and the #1 cause of delays is simply too many planes. Here's why 👇

segments.say: Airlines now carry 5.2 billion passengers a year, nearly double 2010.
On-time arrivals slipped to 74 percent, down from 80.
The biggest cause isn't weather, it's just too many planes, 35 percent of delays.

stories.title: A Third Of All Food Is Never Eaten
stories.hook: We trash a third of all food.
stories.closing: We farm it, then we trash it.
stories.question: What do you throw out most? Drop it below.
stories.caption: Most food waste isn't farms — 43% happens in our own homes. The average American trashes 325 lbs a year, worth $310 billion. Here's the breakdown 👇

segments.say: Most waste isn't farms, it's us. 43 percent happens in our own homes.
The average American now tosses 325 pounds of food a year.
That adds up to about 310 billion dollars scraped straight into the trash.

stories.title: James Webb Rewrote the Universe. 3 Numbers Prove It.
stories.hook: The universe is 10 times bigger than we thought.
stories.closing: Turns out, we had no idea how big it was.
stories.question: Does knowing the universe has 2 trillion galaxies make you feel small — or amazed? Drop it below.
stories.caption: Hubble told us there were 200 billion galaxies. JWST found 2 trillion. It also spotted a galaxy from 290 million years after the Big Bang. And it found a crack in the laws of physics. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: Hubble told us there were about 200 billion galaxies. JWST looked deeper and found closer to 2000 billion — 2 trillion. That's 10 times more than we thought.

segments.topic: oldest confirmed galaxy (million years after Big Bang)
segments.say: JWST keeps breaking its own record. The newest: a galaxy that formed just 290 million years after the Big Bang. The universe is 13,800 million years old. We are watching the very first stars switch on.
Here's the problem. Early-universe physics predicts the expansion rate at 67. JWST direct measurement says 73. That gap — the Hubble Tension — means something in our model of the universe is wrong.

stories.title: Half the World Will Need Glasses by 2050. Here's Why.
stories.hook: The world's eyes are failing. Fast.
stories.closing: Two trillion screens. Fifty percent nearsighted. The math is written on the wall — if you can read it.
stories.question: Do you wear glasses or contacts? When did your vision start going? Drop it below.
stories.caption: In 1990, 22% of the world was nearsighted. Today it's 34% — 2.6 billion people. By 2050, the WHO projects 50%. In East Asia, 80% of young adults already need glasses. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 1990, 22 percent of the world was nearsighted. By 2020 that hit 34 percent — 2.6 billion people. The WHO projects 50 percent by 2050. This is growing faster than any eye disease in history.

segments.topic: myopia rate by world region — young adults (percent)
segments.say: But here's what makes it strange. 80 percent of young adults in East Asia are nearsighted. In the Americas, it's 31 percent. In Africa, just 14. The same species, looking at wildly different numbers.

The scary part is high myopia — the severe kind that risks blindness. It was 3 percent in 2000. It's 8 percent today, and projected to hit 10 percent by 2050. That's 1 billion people at risk of losing their sight.

stories.title: You Think You Work All Day. Here's Where Your Time Actually Goes.
stories.hook: You're not as busy as you think.
stories.closing: 5 hours of leisure a day — mostly screens. Make of that what you will.
stories.question: How many hours of screen time did you log yesterday? Drop the number below.
stories.caption: The average American gets 5 hours of leisure a day. 3.6 of those go to screens. You spend more time on leisure than on work. But only 12 minutes of that leisure is reading. Here's the data 👇

segments.topic: how the average American spends 24 hours
segments.say: Here is where the day actually goes. Sleep wins at 8.7 hours. But leisure — 5 hours — beats out work at 3.4 hours. Most people think they work more than they play. The BLS data disagrees.

segments.topic: screen vs. non-screen leisure (hours per day)
segments.say: Of those 5 leisure hours, screens take 3.2. Non-screen leisure — socializing, reading, exercise — gets just 1.8 hours. Reading alone is 12 minutes. We have more free time than ever, and screens claimed most of it.

segments.topic: daily leisure screen time (hours per day)
segments.say: And it's been climbing. In 2003, screen leisure was 2.8 hours a day. By 2023, it hit 3.6. That's nearly an hour of your daily life that migrated from the physical world to a screen in 20 years.

stories.title: US Wildfires Are Burning 3x More Land Than 30 Years Ago
stories.hook: The West is burning — and it's getting worse.
stories.closing: Every year, the fire season starts earlier and lasts longer. That's not bad luck — it's physics.
stories.question: Have wildfires ever affected where you live? Drop your state below.
stories.caption: In the 1980s, US wildfires burned 2.8 million acres a year. The last decade averaged 7.4 million. The 5 worst years on record all happened after 2000. Here's what the data shows 👇

segments.topic: US wildfire acres burned per year (millions)
segments.say: In the mid-1980s, US wildfires burned roughly 2 to 3 million acres a year. By 2015 and 2020, that reached 10.1 million acres. The trend line is not subtle — it's a straight climb.

segments.topic: average wildfire acres per year by decade (millions)
segments.say: Decade to decade, the shift is undeniable. The 1983 to 1992 average was 2.8 million acres. The 2013 to 2022 average was 7.4 million — nearly 3 times higher. Hotter, drier conditions changed the math.

segments.topic: worst US wildfire years by acres burned (millions)
segments.say: And all 5 of the worst wildfire years in American history happened after 2006. 2015 and 2020 tied at 10.1 million acres. These are not random — they follow the drought map exactly.

stories.title: How Many People Have Ever Lived
stories.hook: Way more people have lived than are alive now.
stories.closing: Everyone you'll ever meet is a sliver of everyone who ever was.
stories.question: Does 117 billion surprise you? Drop a thought below.
stories.caption: About 117 billion humans have ever been born — and the 8 billion alive today are just ~7% of them. It took all of history to reach 1 billion, then we added 7 billion in 200 years. Here's the breakdown 👇

segments.say: About 117 billion people have ever been born.
The 8 billion alive today are only about 7 percent of everyone.
It took all of history to reach 1 billion — then we added 7 billion in 200 years.

stories.title: The Deadliest Animal On Earth
stories.hook: The deadliest animal isn't what you think.
stories.closing: The thing most likely to kill you is the size of a fingernail.
stories.question: Which one surprised you? Drop it below.
stories.caption: Sharks kill about 10 people a year. Mosquitoes kill over 700,000 — mostly through malaria. Here's what actually kills humans 👇

segments.say: Mosquitoes kill more than 700,000 people a year — sharks barely 10.
Most of it is malaria — about 600,000 deaths a year.
Snakes alone kill over 100,000 — the animals we fear most barely register.

stories.title: You'll Spend 8 Years of Your Life on Your Phone
stories.hook: You'll spend nearly a decade of your life staring at a screen.
stories.closing: That's not screen time. That's life time.
stories.question: How many hours is your screen time? Be honest below.
stories.caption: The average person spends about 7 hours a day looking at screens — that adds up to roughly 8 years of a lifetime. Here's where it all goes 👇

segments.say: People now average around 7 hours a day on screens — in some countries it's over 9.
Most of it is social media and video — about four and a half hours a day combined.
Over a lifetime that's roughly 8 years on screens — more than you'll spend eating or socializing.

stories.title: The Most Spoken Languages on Earth
stories.hook: More people speak English than any language — but most of them learned it.
stories.closing: The world speaks thousands of languages. Half of everyone uses just a handful.
stories.question: What languages do you speak? Drop them below.
stories.caption: About 1.5 billion people speak English — but only ~380 million as a native tongue. The rest learned it. Here's how the world really talks 👇

segments.say: English leads with about 1.5 billion total speakers — most learned it as a second language.
By native speakers it flips — Mandarin comes first, and English drops to third.
Online, English dominates — more than half of all websites are written in it.

stories.title: Having a Baby in America Costs 30x More Than in the UK
stories.hook: Same baby. Wildly different bill.
stories.closing: Same medical event, same outcome — the only thing that really changes is the country printed on the bill.
stories.question: If you've had a kid, what did your bill actually look like? Drop it below.
stories.caption: The average US hospital birth is billed around $19,000 — up from $9,200 in 2010. In the UK it's about $600. In Spain, $400. Same baby, very different bill. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 2010, the average US hospital birth was billed at about 9,200 dollars. By 2017 it was 13,800. By 2024, roughly 19,000 dollars — more than double in 14 years.

segments.topic: average cost to give birth, by country ($)
segments.say: Now compare that to the rest of the world. The US bill is around 19,000 dollars. Switzerland, 7,900. Germany, about 2,100. The UK? Roughly 600. Spain, just 400. Same medical event.

segments.topic: out-of-pocket birth cost, even with insurance ($)
segments.say: And insurance doesn't make it free. On a high-deductible plan, parents still pay about 4,500 dollars out of pocket. An employer PPO, around 2,900. Only Medicaid brings it close to zero.

stories.title: Infant Daycare Now Costs More Than Rent
stories.hook: Daycare quietly became the biggest bill you never planned for.
stories.closing: For millions of parents, going to work barely covers the cost of someone else watching their kid while they do it.
stories.question: What does daycare cost where you live? Drop your state below.
stories.caption: Average US infant daycare runs about $16,800 a year — more than median rent, and more than in-state public college. For a minimum-wage worker, that's 64% of income. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 2000, a year of infant daycare averaged about 5,800 dollars. By 2010, 9,900. Today, around 16,800 dollars a year — nearly triple in a generation.

segments.topic: annual US cost, daycare vs other big bills ($)
segments.say: Put that next to your other big bills. Infant daycare, 16,800 dollars. Median rent, 15,600. In-state public college, 11,000. A year of daycare now costs more than a year of college.

segments.topic: infant care as a share of income (%)
segments.say: And it lands hardest on the people with the least. For a minimum-wage worker, infant care eats 64 percent of income. For a single parent, 36 percent. Even a median family spends 13 percent.

stories.title: The Cheetah Isn't the Fastest Animal on Earth
stories.hook: The cheetah isn't the fastest animal. Not even close.
stories.closing: On land you're fast. In the sky and sea, you're barely on the chart.
stories.question: Which speed surprised you most? Drop it below.
stories.caption: The cheetah tops out at 70 mph on land — but a peregrine falcon dives at 240 mph, and a sailfish hits 68 mph underwater. Here's the full speed breakdown 👇

segments.say: On land, the cheetah tops at 70 miles per hour, barely faster than a highway speed limit. The pronghorn antelope, America's fastest animal, keeps pace at 55.
In the air, it isn't close. The peregrine falcon dives at 240 miles per hour in a hunting stoop. The cheetah — fastest thing on land — would look slow next to a racing pigeon.
In the water, the sailfish hits 68 miles per hour — faster than the cheetah. An Olympic swimmer goes about 5. The ocean has its own top gear.

stories.title: The Arctic Is Melting Faster Than Anyone Predicted
stories.hook: The Arctic lost an area bigger than Alaska. Already.
stories.closing: The oldest, thickest ice is nearly gone. What's left is young, thin, and melting faster every year.
stories.question: Does the Arctic ice collapse change how you think about climate? Drop it below.
stories.caption: Arctic sea ice September minimum fell from 7.2M sq km in 1980 to 4.2M in 2023 — a loss bigger than Alaska. Multi-year ice dropped from 61% to just 13% of the total. Here's the data 👇

segments.topic: Arctic September ice extent (million sq km)
segments.say: In 1980, Arctic sea ice reached 7.2 million square kilometers at its September minimum. By 2012 it hit a record low of 3.4 million. In 2023, it sat at 4.2 — about half what it was when your parents were born.

But the real story is what kind of ice is left. In 1985, 61 percent of Arctic ice was old, thick, multi-year ice that had survived summer melts. By 2023, that fell to just 13 percent. The durable ice is nearly gone.

segments.topic: Arctic ice remaining vs lost since 1980 (million sq km)
segments.say: Put it together: 3 million square kilometers of ice — an area bigger than Alaska — has simply vanished since 1980. Of the original 7.2 million, only 4.2 million remains. The baseline is gone.

stories.title: More Than Half the World Now Lives in Cities
stories.hook: For the first time in history, cities beat the countryside.
stories.closing: We crossed the line in 2007. Most humans now live in cities — and that number keeps climbing.
stories.question: City or countryside? Where would you rather be? Drop it below.
stories.caption: In 1950, just 30% of the world lived in cities. In 2007 it crossed 50% for the first time. By 2025, 58% — and the number of megacities (10M+ people) grew from 2 to 37. Here's the data 👇

segments.topic: share of world population in urban areas (%)
segments.say: In 1950, just 30 percent of the world lived in cities. By 2007 it crossed 50 percent for the first time in human history. Today it's 58 and rising. We became an urban species within one lifetime.

Some places are essentially already all city. Singapore and Monaco hit 100 percent. Kuwait, 99. Belgium, 98. The United States is at 83. The world average, still just 58 — the gap between rich and developing worlds is enormous.
And the giants keep multiplying. In 1950 only two cities held 10 million or more people. By 2000 there were 17. Today there are 37 megacities. We're building new ones faster than we built cathedrals.

stories.title: Humans Used to Die at 32. Then This Happened.
stories.hook: Humans used to die at 32. What changed everything?
stories.closing: Medicine didn't just add years. It changed what we die from. You're living in the longest era of human life in history.
stories.question: What age do you expect to live to? Drop it below.
stories.caption: In 1900, global life expectancy was 32. Today it's 73. Japan's average is 84.3. And the cause of death flipped: infectious diseases used to be 50% of deaths — today chronic diseases are 74%. Here's what happened 👇

segments.say: In 1900, the average human lived to 32. By 1950 it was 48. Today it's 73 — more than doubled in one century. Vaccines, antibiotics, and clean water did more for humanity than almost anything else in history.

But the gains are not equal. Japan averages 84.3 years, Switzerland 83.8. The United States, 78.4 — below most wealthy nations. Nigeria, 55.2. You can gain or lose 30 years of life just based on where you're born.

segments.topic: cause of death: infectious vs chronic (%)
segments.say: And what kills us changed completely. In 1900, infectious diseases — tuberculosis, cholera, pneumonia — caused about half of all deaths. Today, chronic diseases like heart disease and cancer account for 74 percent. We beat the old killers. We inherited new ones.

stories.title: Insurers Are Quietly Abandoning Entire States
stories.hook: In some places, you can barely buy home insurance at any price.
stories.closing: As disasters get more expensive, insurers are doing the math — and in the riskiest places, they're simply walking away.
stories.question: Has your home insurance jumped or been dropped? Tell us where you live.
stories.caption: Average US home insurance hit about $2,400 a year — double 2019. In Florida it's around $11,000. And the share of homeowners dropped by their insurer reached 4% in 2024. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 2019, the average US home insurance policy cost about 1,200 dollars a year. By 2023, 1,900. By 2025, around 2,400 dollars — double in just six years.

segments.topic: average home insurance premium by state ($)
segments.say: But the average hides the extremes. In Florida, the typical premium is around 11,000 dollars. Louisiana, 6,300. Texas, 4,400. The national average is 2,400 — and in Vermont, just 900.

segments.topic: share of homeowners dropped by their insurer (%)
segments.say: And increasingly, insurers just say no. In 2018, about 1.5 percent of homeowners were dropped at renewal. By 2024, that doubled to 4 percent — concentrated exactly where disaster risk is highest.

stories.title: Your Morning Cup Is the World's Most Popular Drug
stories.hook: Your morning cup is the most popular drug on Earth.
stories.closing: Three cups and counting. No prescription required.

segments.say: In 2000 the average American drank fewer than 2 cups a day. By 2024 that's 3.2. We've been quietly dosing up for 25 years straight.
A single large energy drink hits 160 mg. Cold brew edges it at 155. One espresso is 128. The FDA flags anything over 200 mg at once as a risk.
The FDA says 400 mg a day is the safe ceiling. The average American clears half that. Heavy users hit 580 — clearing the limit by nearly 50 percent.

stories.question: How many cups are you actually drinking today?

stories.title: Your Commute Is Longer Than Your Parents' Was
stories.hook: Your commute is longer than your parents' was.
stories.closing: More minutes, same destination. The commute always wins.

segments.say: In 1980 the average American commute was 22 minutes one-way. By 2023 it's 28. Small number — but that's 6 extra minutes every single workday, forever.
New York City averages 41 minutes each way. San Francisco 34, Chicago 33. If you're in a major metro, your commute is already its own part-time job.

segments.topic: annual hours in commute vs. vacation
segments.say: The average commuter loses 237 hours a year in transit. That's triple the vacation days most Americans actually take. NYC commuters burn 342 hours — six full weeks.

stories.question: How long is your commute? Drop it below.

stories.title: 40% of All Languages Have Fewer Than 1,000 Speakers
stories.hook: 40% of all languages have under 1,000 speakers.
stories.closing: Seven thousand worlds of words. Most will never be written down.

segments.say: In the 1950s about 5 languages went extinct each decade. By the 2010s that number hit 34. The pace of language loss has increased sevenfold in 70 years.
Of roughly 7,100 languages alive today, 3,100 have under a thousand speakers. That's nearly half of all human languages — each a complete world — on the edge of silence.

segments.topic: share of world population by language tier
segments.say: The top 10 languages cover 41% of all humans. The remaining 6,500 languages share just 8% of the world's population — spread impossibly thin across shrinking communities.

stories.question: Do you speak a language that could disappear?

stories.title: Most Americans Are One Bad Day From Broke
stories.hook: Most Americans are one bad day from broke.
stories.closing: The math is simple. The savings aren't.

segments.say: In 2010, 49% of Americans had a three-month emergency fund. By 2024 that's down to 34%. Fifteen years of gains — erased. And that was before inflation hit.
Earn over 150K? 78% are covered. Earn under 30K? Only 12%. Emergency funds aren't a savings problem — they're an income problem hiding inside a savings problem.
A proper three-month cushion costs about 9,900 dollars. The median American has 2,100 in liquid savings. That's a 7,800-dollar gap between where you are and where you'd be safe.

stories.question: Do you have a full emergency fund? Real talk below.

stories.title: The Average US Wedding Now Costs More Than a New Car
stories.hook: Saying "I do" got expensive.
stories.closing: The party lasts one night — the price tag now rivals a car or a year of college.
stories.question: What did your wedding actually cost? Drop it below.
stories.caption: The average US wedding hit about $35,000 in 2024 — up from $20,000 in 2000 — and in Manhattan it's closer to $63,000. Here's where the money goes 👇

segments.say: In 2000, the average US wedding cost about 20,000 dollars. By 2015 it was 31,000. Today it's around 35,000 dollars — more than the price of a new car.
And here's where it goes. The venue alone averages 12,800 dollars. Catering, 8,500. The band or DJ, 4,500. Photography, 3,200. The flowers cost more than the dress.
But where you marry changes everything. In Manhattan the average is 63,000 dollars. San Francisco, 45,000. The national average is 35,000 — and in the rural Midwest, just 21,000.

stories.title: Why Chocolate Is About to Get Much More Expensive
stories.hook: Your favorite chocolate bar is in trouble.
stories.closing: When two countries grow most of the world's cocoa and their crops fail, the whole planet's chocolate gets more expensive.
stories.question: Have you noticed chocolate getting pricier? Tell us below.
stories.caption: Cocoa hit about $10,000 a ton in 2024 — more than triple the year before — because most of it comes from just two countries. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: For years, cocoa traded around 3,000 dollars a ton. Then in 2024 it exploded to about 10,000 dollars — more than triple, the highest price in history.
And here's the problem. The Ivory Coast grows 38 percent of the world's cocoa. Ghana, another 17. Just two countries supply over half the planet — so when their harvests fail, everyone pays.
You're already seeing it. A chocolate bar that cost about 1.30 in 2019 is now around 2.20 — and with cocoa this high, it isn't coming back down soon.

stories.title: Concert Tickets Cost Triple What They Did in 2010
stories.hook: Seeing your favorite artist got brutal.
stories.closing: Between face value and fees, a night out at a concert now costs what a weekend used to.
stories.question: What's the most you've paid for a ticket? Drop it below.
stories.caption: The average top-tour concert ticket went from about $45 in 2010 to $135 in 2024 — before fees add another 40%. Here's the breakdown 👇

segments.say: In 2010, the average top-tour concert ticket cost about 45 dollars. By 2020 it was 96. In 2024, around 135 dollars — triple what it was in 2010.
But the sticker price is a lie. On a 90 dollar ticket, the service fee adds 28 dollars. Facility fee, 6. Processing, 5. Delivery, 4 — nearly 40 percent on top.
And for the hot shows, resale is worse. In 2015, tickets resold for about 60 percent over face. By 2024, 180 percent — nearly triple the printed price.

stories.title: Americans Now Spend a Record Amount on Their Pets
stories.hook: Pets became a serious line item.
stories.closing: For a lot of households, the dog is officially a member of the family — budget and all.
stories.question: How much do you spend on your pet a year? Tell us below.
stories.caption: US pet spending hit about $152 billion in 2024 — triple what it was in 2010 — with a large dog now running ~$1,800 a year. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 2010, Americans spent about 48 billion dollars on their pets. By 2020, 104 billion. In 2024, around 152 billion — more than triple in 14 years.
Food and treats lead at 64 billion dollars. Vet care and products, 47 billion. Supplies and medications, 32 billion. Grooming and boarding, another 12.
Per animal, it adds up fast. A large dog runs about 1,800 dollars a year. A small dog, 1,200. A cat, 1,100. Even a bird is around 350 a year.

stories.title: The Silent War We're Losing: Superbugs
stories.hook: Antibiotics are running out of time — and so are we.
stories.closing: The drugs that saved the 20th century are losing the 21st.
stories.question: Did you know antibiotic approvals have dropped this much? Comment below.
stories.caption: AMR killed 1.27 million people in 2019 — and the antibiotic pipeline has nearly dried up. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 2000, drug-resistant bacteria killed about 700 thousand people worldwide. By 2019, that number hit 1.27 million. By 2023, 1.35 million — more than HIV and malaria combined.
The deadliest culprits: E. coli kills 216 thousand a year. MRSA, 170 thousand. Klebsiella pneumoniae, 143 thousand. Together, just these six pathogens account for most AMR deaths globally.
And the pipeline is drying up. In the 1980s, the FDA approved 16 new antibiotics. By the 2000s, just 7. This decade so far: 4. Bacteria evolve faster than we innovate.

stories.title: Why Finland Is the Happiest Country on Earth
stories.hook: Finland has been the world's happiest country for 7 years straight. Why?
stories.closing: The data says what happiness looks like. Now you know.
stories.question: What do you think matters most for national happiness? Drop it below.
stories.caption: Finland scores 7.74 out of 10 on the World Happiness Report — the US is 23rd. Here's the breakdown 👇

segments.say: Finland's happiness score has risen almost every year since 2015 — from 7.41 to a peak of 7.84 in 2021. Even after a slight dip, Finland sits at 7.74 in 2024. It has topped the global rankings every year since 2018.
The top ten are dominated by Nordic countries. Finland 7.74, Denmark 7.58, Iceland 7.53, Sweden 7.35. The United States? 23rd, at 6.72 — well below the Nordic cluster.

segments.topic: Finland vs US vs world average happiness score
segments.say: Against a world average of 5.51, both Finland and the US are well above average. But Finland's 7.74 versus the US's 6.72 is a 1-point gap — enormous on this scale. Researchers point to social trust, safety nets, and work-life balance as the difference-makers.

stories.title: 97% of Earth's Water Is Useless to You
stories.hook: The planet is covered in water. Almost none of it is drinkable.
stories.closing: The blue planet has a freshwater problem. And it's getting worse.
stories.question: Did you know water access was this unequal? Comment below.
stories.caption: Only 0.3% of Earth's water is accessible freshwater — and 2.2 billion people still lack safe drinking water. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: Of all the water on Earth, 97 percent is saltwater in the oceans. Two percent is locked in ice caps and glaciers. Groundwater accounts for 0.7 percent — most too deep or contaminated. Only 0.3 percent is actually accessible freshwater. That's all we have.

And billions of people can't reliably access even that 0.3 percent. In 2015, 2.5 billion lacked safely managed drinking water. Progress has been slow: by 2022, still 2.2 billion — that's one in four people on Earth.

The worst-hit regions: Bahrain scores a perfect 5 on WRI's water stress index — every drop of rainfall is over-allocated. Kuwait, Qatar, Cyprus all near the top. Pakistan, home to 230 million people, is close behind. These countries are already running on empty.

stories.title: Space Is Getting Crowded Fast
stories.hook: There are 9,000 satellites above your head — and it only just started.
stories.closing: We launched a space age without a space traffic plan.
stories.question: Are you worried about space debris? Drop your take below.
stories.caption: The number of active satellites shot from 1,400 in 2015 to 9,000 by 2024 — mostly thanks to one company. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 2000, about 600 active satellites orbited Earth. In 2015, 1,400. Then Starlink launched — and by 2021, 4,550. By 2023, 7,500. By 2024, roughly 9,000. More were launched in the last five years than in the previous 60 combined.

One company dominates completely: SpaceX operates roughly 6,500 Starlink satellites — nearly three-quarters of everything in orbit. OneWeb is a distant second at 630. Planet Labs, Spire, SES, and Intelsat account for most of the rest.

But for every active satellite, there are three pieces of tracked debris. Space Command tracks 27,500 objects large enough to destroy a spacecraft. Estimated debris pieces over one centimeter: 130,000. A single collision can shatter into thousands of new fragments — the cascade known as Kessler Syndrome.

stories.title: Half the World's Coral Is Already Gone
stories.hook: Half the world's coral reefs are already gone. Quietly.
stories.closing: The reef took ten thousand years to build. We've undone half of it in fifty.
stories.question: Did you know coral reefs were disappearing this fast? Drop it below.
stories.caption: In 2023, 56% of the world's reefs experienced bleaching — the fourth global mass bleaching event ever. The Caribbean has lost 80% of its coral since the 1970s. Here's the data 👇

segments.topic: share of reefs bleaching per year (%)
segments.say: In 1998, the first global mass bleaching hit about 11 percent of reefs. By 2016 it was 46 percent — nearly half the world's coral, in a single year. In 2023, 56 percent. That's more than half of every reef on Earth, bleaching in one year.
Zooming out: since the 1970s, about half of all coral cover on Earth has disappeared. A reef that took ten thousand years to grow — halved in fifty.

segments.topic: coral cover lost since 1970s by region (%)
segments.say: But the losses aren't even. The Florida Keys have lost 90 percent of their coral. The Caribbean, 80 percent. Hawaii, 60. Even the Great Barrier Reef — Australia's national icon — has lost half its cover.

stories.title: The US Locks Up More People Than Any Country on Earth
stories.hook: The US has 5% of the world's people. And 20% of its prisoners.
stories.closing: No other rich country comes close. The question isn't how many — it's why.
stories.question: What do you think drives the US incarceration rate? Drop it below.
stories.caption: The US incarcerates 630 per 100,000 people — four times the world average. The rate rose sevenfold from 1972 to its peak in 2008. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 1972, the US imprisoned 161 people per 100,000. By 1980 it was 221. Then the War on Drugs hit — and by 2008, 755 per 100,000. The rate nearly quintupled in 36 years. Even after declining, it sits at 608 today.

segments.topic: incarceration rate: US vs world benchmarks (per 100,000)
segments.say: Now compare that to the world. The US locks up 630 per 100,000. Russia, 328. The world average is 155. Western Europe averages around 100. The US rate is four times the global average — and six times Western Europe.

The US isn't just high — it leads the world. At 630 per 100,000, it beats Turkmenistan, El Salvador, and Cuba. The only company it keeps are authoritarian states. No other democracy is in the same bracket.

stories.title: Americans Are Quietly Leaving California and New York
stories.hook: More Americans are leaving California than any state in history.
stories.closing: The map of where Americans live is redrawing itself — one moving truck at a time.
stories.question: Have you moved states in the last 5 years? Where'd you go? Drop it below.
stories.caption: In 2023, California lost 361,000 people to other states — and Florida gained 315,000. Only 57% of Americans still live where they were born. Here's the data 👇

segments.topic: top states by net domestic out-migration, 2023 (people)
segments.say: In 2023, California lost 361,000 more residents to other states than it gained. New York lost 209,000. Illinois, 102,000. Massachusetts and New Jersey round out the top five. These are the states people are leaving.

segments.topic: top states by net domestic in-migration, 2023 (people)
segments.say: And here's where they're going. Florida gained 315,000. Texas, 290,000. North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Georgia — all in the top seven. Sun Belt states are absorbing the exodus.

segments.topic: share of Americans still living in birth state (%)
segments.say: And Americans are increasingly willing to move. In 1980, 68 percent of Americans still lived in the state where they were born. Today that's 57 percent — the lowest ever. One in three Americans has voted with their feet.

stories.title: The Minimum Wage Is Worth Less Than in 1968
stories.hook: The federal minimum wage hasn't changed since 2009. Here's what that actually means.
stories.closing: The number on the check went up. The number of things you can buy went down.
stories.question: Should the federal minimum wage be raised? Drop your number below.
stories.caption: The federal minimum wage peaked in 1968 at $1.60/hr — worth $14.63 today. It's been stuck at $7.25 since 2009. Here's what that gap actually looks like 👇

segments.say: In 1968, the federal minimum wage was $1.60 an hour — which is $14.63 in today's dollars. That's the peak. By 2000 the real value had fallen to $7.35. It briefly rose with a 2009 raise to $7.25 — but since then, inflation has eroded it back to the nominal. Today's minimum wage buys less than at any point since the 1940s.

segments.topic: current federal minimum vs. 1968 peak in 2024 dollars ($/hr)
segments.say: Here's the gap: the current federal minimum is $7.25. The 1968 equivalent in today's dollars is $14.63. That means minimum wage workers today earn about half what their 1968 counterparts could buy. Same job. Half the purchasing power.

segments.topic: state minimum wages vs. federal floor ($/hr, 2024)
segments.say: States have stepped in where the federal government hasn't. California is at $16.50, Washington $16.28, New York $16. Massachusetts, $15. But 20 states still use the federal floor — $7.25 — unchanged since 2009.

stories.title: The World's Tallest Buildings Keep Getting Taller
stories.hook: Some buildings are half a mile tall.
stories.closing: We keep finding ways to build higher — the record has more than doubled in a single lifetime.
stories.question: Would you go up to the top? Drop a floor number.
stories.caption: The Burj Khalifa is 828m — nearly twice the Empire State Building, and the record has doubled since 1931. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: The Burj Khalifa towers at 828 meters. The Merdeka 118 in Kuala Lumpur, 679. Shanghai Tower, 632. These are the tallest structures humans have ever built.
Put it next to what you know. The Burj is 828 meters. The Empire State Building, 443. The Eiffel Tower, 330. The Statue of Liberty barely registers at 93.
And it keeps climbing. In 1931 the record was 381 meters. By 2004, 508. Then the Burj smashed it to 828 — more than double, in under a century.

stories.title: Gas Costs Five Times More in Some Countries
stories.hook: Your gas might be a bargain.
stories.closing: Same fuel, wildly different price — mostly down to how each country taxes it.
stories.question: What do you pay per gallon where you live?
stories.caption: Gas runs about $3.50/gal in the US but $8.50 in Norway. Same fuel, very different bill — here's why 👇

segments.say: Norway pays about 8.50 a gallon. Germany, 7.40. The UK, 7. The US? Just 3.50 — among the cheapest in the developed world.
And what you drive decides the damage. Filling a full-size SUV runs about 95 dollars. A pickup, 88. A sedan, 52. A motorcycle, just 14.
US prices have been a roller coaster — 1.50 in 2000, nearly 5 dollars at the 2022 peak, back to about 3.50 today.

stories.title: Your Electric Bill Depends Wildly on Your State
stories.hook: Same power. Triple the price.
stories.closing: Where you plug in matters almost as much as how much you use.
stories.question: What's your rate per kilowatt-hour? Drop your state.
stories.caption: Electricity is ~44¢/kWh in Hawaii but 11¢ in Washington. Same power, 4x the price. Here's the map 👇

segments.say: In Hawaii, power costs about 44 cents a kilowatt-hour. California, 31. New York, 23. But in Washington it's just 11 — a four-fold gap across one country.

segments.topic: yearly cost to run an appliance ($)
segments.say: And some things quietly eat your bill. Charging an EV runs about 600 dollars a year. Air conditioning, 320. The water heater, 290. A laptop? Ten.
And it keeps creeping up — from about 9.5 cents in 2005 to 17 today, far faster than it used to.

stories.title: How Much Hidden Water Is in Your Food
stories.hook: You drink way more water than you think.
stories.closing: The water you sip is nothing next to the water it took to grow your dinner.
stories.question: Which one surprised you most?
stories.caption: It takes ~15,400 liters of water to make 1kg of beef, vs 320 for vegetables. The water in your food dwarfs what you drink. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: It takes about 15,400 liters of water to produce one kilo of beef. Cheese, 5,000. Chicken, 4,300. Vegetables? Just 320. The gap is staggering.
Per item it adds up fast. One hamburger hides about 2,400 liters of water. A pizza, 1,260. A single egg, 200. Even an apple, 125.

segments.topic: food water vs the water you drink (L/day)
segments.say: Here's the kicker. You drink about 3 liters of water a day — but the food you eat took around 3,000 liters to produce. A thousand to one.

stories.title: Theme Park Tickets Have Gotten Absurd
stories.hook: A day at the park isn't cheap anymore.
stories.closing: The magic is the same — the price tag is from another planet.
stories.question: What's the most you've paid for a park day?
stories.caption: A one-day Disneyland ticket hit ~$206 — up from $3.50 in 1971 — and a family day runs over $1,300. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: A single day at Disneyland now runs about 206 dollars. Disney World, 189. Universal, 184. Even Six Flags will run you 90.

segments.topic: a park day for a family of 4 ($)
segments.say: But the ticket is just the start. For a family of four: 750 in tickets, 400 for a hotel, 160 on food, 35 to park — over 1,300 dollars for one day.
And it's exploded. A Disney day cost 3.50 in 1971. By 2000, 43. Today, 189 — that's more than 50 times higher.

stories.title: Giraffes Are Secretly Fast
stories.hook: You won't believe how quickly giraffes can run
stories.closing: Giraffes are way faster than you think

segments.say: A giraffe can run 35 miles per hour, a lion 30, and a zebra 25
A giraffe's legs are 6 feet long, while a horse's are 3 feet and a human's are 2.5 feet
Giraffes can run for 10 miles without stopping, while horses can run for 5 miles and humans for 2 miles

stories.question: What's the fastest land animal you've seen in person?

stories.title: The Longest Migration
stories.hook: You won't believe how far some animals migrate each year
stories.closing: Some animals migrate thousands of miles each year

segments.say: The arctic tern migrates 44,000 miles, the caribou 3,000 miles, and the monarch butterfly 2,000 miles
The arctic tern migrates from the Arctic to the Antarctic, the caribou from Alaska to Canada, and the monarch butterfly from Canada to Mexico
The arctic tern takes 50 days to migrate, the caribou 30 days, and the monarch butterfly 20 days

stories.question: What's the longest journey you've ever taken?

stories.title: The Deepest Ocean
stories.hook: You won't believe how deep the ocean is
stories.closing: The ocean is way deeper than you think

segments.say: The Mariana Trench is 36,000 feet deep, the Tonga Trench is 35,000 feet deep, and the Kermadec Trench is 32,000 feet deep
The Mariana Trench has 5 distinct zones, the Tonga Trench has 4, and the Kermadec Trench has 3
The pressure at the bottom of the Mariana Trench is 1,086 pounds per square inch, the Tonga Trench is 1,050 pounds per square inch, and the Kermadec Trench is 1,000 pounds per square inch

stories.question: What's the deepest place you've ever been?

stories.title: The Largest Living Thing
stories.hook: You won't believe what the largest living thing is
stories.closing: The largest living thing is way bigger than you think

segments.say: The Armillaria ostoyae fungus covers 2,200 acres, the Great Barrier Reef covers 133,000 acres, and the Blue Whale is 0.02 acres
The Armillaria ostoyae fungus is 2,400 years old, the Great Barrier Reef is 20 million years old, and the Blue Whale is 10-30 years old
The Armillaria ostoyae fungus weighs 35,000 tons, the Great Barrier Reef weighs 2.5 billion tons, and the Blue Whale weighs 150-170 tons

stories.title: Some Animals Live 500 Years. Here's the Scale.
stories.hook: A shark alive before the American Revolution is still swimming.
stories.closing: You're a mayfly compared to a Greenland shark.
stories.question: Which animal lifespan surprised you most? Drop it below.
stories.caption: A Greenland shark alive during the American Revolution is probably still out there. The ocean quahog clam hit 507 years old. Meanwhile you have a dog that might make 15. The range of animal lifespans is almost impossible to grasp. 👇

segments.say: A house mouse tops out at 4 years. A Greenland shark can hit 400. The ocean quahog clam — 507 years. That clam was alive during the Crusades. The range of animal lifespans is almost impossible to process.
There's a rough rule: bigger body, longer life. A blue whale averages 90 years; an elephant 65; a mouse 2. Bigger animals run slower metabolisms — fewer heartbeats per lifetime, slower cellular wear.

segments.topic: outliers vs expected lifespan for their size
segments.say: Then there are the cheaters. The naked mole rat weighs 35 grams — same as a mouse — but lives 31 years. Ten times longer. Cold deep water, low oxygen, slow metabolism: the Greenland shark uses all three tricks at once. Age is just a number, apparently.

stories.title: One Volcano Darkened the World for a Year
stories.hook: One eruption cancelled summer for the entire planet.
stories.closing: Tambora sneezed. The Northern Hemisphere shivered for two years.
stories.question: Had you heard of the Year Without Summer before? Comment below.
stories.caption: In April 1815, Mount Tambora erupted — the most powerful volcanic blast in recorded history. The sulfur it shot into the stratosphere dimmed sunlight globally. 1816 had no summer: crops failed in Europe and North America, killing 130,000 people. 👇

segments.topic: Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI) of famous eruptions
segments.say: The Volcanic Explosivity Index is logarithmic — each step is 10 times more powerful. Krakatoa was a 6. Mount St. Helens was a 5. Tambora, in April 1815, was a 7 — ten times stronger than Krakatoa and the most powerful eruption in recorded human history.

The eruption blasted sulfur into the stratosphere, forming a haze that dimmed sunlight worldwide. In 1816 — the Year Without Summer — Northern Hemisphere temperatures dropped 0.7°C below normal. Frosts hit New England in July. Snow fell in June in parts of Europe.

Pinatubo in 1991 killed 800 people. Krakatoa killed 36,000. Tambora's total — direct deaths plus the famine and disease that followed — reached 130,000. It is the deadliest volcanic event in recorded history.

stories.title: Hollywood Is Losing the Seat War
stories.hook: Americans bought 700 million fewer movie tickets than in 2002.

stories.question: When did you last go to a movie theater? Drop the year below.
stories.caption: In 2002, Americans bought 1.58 billion movie tickets. By 2024 that number was down to 855 million — a 46% drop. And the industry's response (raise ticket prices) made it worse. Meanwhile Netflix added 300 million subscribers. Here's the data. 👇

segments.say: In 2002, Americans bought 1,579 million movie tickets — the all-time peak. By 2019, before COVID, that had already slid to 1,228 million. The pandemic cratered it to 223 million. By 2024 it only recovered to 855 million. Still 46% below the peak, after 22 years.

Hollywood's response: raise prices. The average ticket went from $5.39 in 2000 to $11.42 in 2024. More than double. That covers some revenue loss on paper — but it also gave people one more reason to stay home.

segments.topic: US admissions vs the 2002 peak (million tickets)
segments.say: To put it plainly: in 2024 Americans bought 855 million tickets. In 2002 they bought 1,579 million. That's 724 million seats that stayed empty — roughly one ticket for every American, every year, that Hollywood no longer sells.

stories.title: Glaciers Are Melting Faster Every Decade
stories.hook: Glaciers lost 70% more ice per year in 2020 than in 1970.
stories.closing: The mountains are shrinking. The oceans are filling.
stories.question: Have you ever seen a glacier in person? Drop below.
stories.caption: Glaciers worldwide (not counting Greenland and Antarctica) lost 219 Gt of ice per year in the 1970s. By the 2010s that rate hit 370 Gt per year — 70% faster. Alaska and the Arctic are losing the most. It's already raising sea levels 0.74 mm every year. 👇

segments.topic: global glacier mass loss (Gt per year)
segments.say: In the 1970s, glaciers worldwide lost 219 Gt of ice per year — already alarming. By the 2010s, that rate hit 370 Gt annually. That's 70% faster in 50 years. Each gigaton is 1 billion metric tons of water added to the ocean.

Alaska loses 73 Gt per year — the most of any single region. Arctic Canada follows at 59, then the Greenland periphery at 47, and High Mountain Asia — the Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau — at 42. The water towers of the world are draining.

segments.topic: sea level rise contribution by source (mm/yr)
segments.say: All that melt has to go somewhere. Glaciers outside the ice sheets now raise sea levels 0.74 mm per year. Add the Greenland ice sheet at 0.77 and Antarctica at 0.43, and ice alone adds over 1.9 mm a year — on top of thermal expansion from warming seas. It compounds.

stories.title: Grown Kids Never Left. Here's Why.
stories.hook: Grown kids never left. Here's why.
stories.closing: Moving out got expensive. Moving back got normal.
stories.question: Do you live with extended family right now? Tell us below.
stories.caption: A quarter of Americans 25 to 34 now live with their parents or grandparents — more than double the rate in 1971. It's not culture. It's money. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: In 1971, only 7 percent of Americans lived with extended family under one roof. By 2021, that number hit 18 percent — the highest share in decades. Something reversed.
Look closer, and it's young adults driving it. In 1971, just 9 percent of 25 to 34 year-olds lived with family. By 2021, 25 percent did — more than double. Grown kids are staying put.
So why? Ask people living it, and 57 percent say money is the main reason — rent, debt, or a paycheck that doesn't stretch far enough. This isn't a lifestyle trend. It's a budget decision.

stories.title: Colorblind Doesn't Mean What You Think
stories.hook: Colorblind almost never means what you think.
stories.closing: Most 'colorblind' guys just see less green — not zero color.
stories.question: Do you know anyone who's colorblind? Tell us below.
stories.caption: 8 percent of men can't fully tell certain reds from greens — only 0.5 percent of women share that. But most 'colorblind' people still see color; true total colorblindness is about 2,500 times rarer. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: Eight percent of men have red-green color blindness. For women, it's only 0.5 percent — 16 times rarer. Red and green vision genes sit on the X chromosome, and men only get one copy.

segments.topic: type of color blindness (share of men)
segments.say: Most cases aren't full colorblindness. 5 percent of men have deuteranomaly — weak green vision, not missing green. The rarer forms — protanomaly, protanopia, deuteranopia — each affect about 1 percent. One glitch, four results.

So what about seeing zero color? That's achromatopsia — roughly 1 in 30,000 people. Compare that to the 8 percent of men with some red-green deficiency. True colorblindness is about 2,500 times rarer.

stories.title: The Sun's Surface Is Colder Than Its Air
stories.hook: The Sun's surface is the cold part.
stories.closing: Somewhere out past the Sun's 'cool' surface, it gets hot again.
stories.question: Did you know the Sun's atmosphere is hotter than its surface? Tell us below.
stories.caption: The Sun's surface is 'only' 5,500 degrees Celsius — but its outer atmosphere, the corona, is over 2 million degrees. Scientists call it the coronal heating problem. Here's the data 👇

segments.say: You'd expect the Sun to cool as you leave its 15 million degree core. The surface — the photosphere — is 'only' 5,500 degrees. Then the corona, further out, hits 2 million degrees again.

That's the corona running 500 times hotter than the surface beneath it — like stepping from a fire and feeling it get hotter. Scientists call this the coronal heating problem. It stumped them for decades.

segments.topic: Parker Solar Probe distance from the Sun
segments.say: NASA's Parker Solar Probe flew closer to find out — 15 million miles out in 2018, 8 million in 2021, 3.8 million by 2024. Flying through the corona, it found magnetic waves doing the heating.

stories.title: Why Some Animals Barely Sleep at All
stories.hook: A koala sleeps 22 hours. A giraffe barely 2.
stories.closing: Sleep isn't laziness. For a giraffe, it's survival math.
stories.question: How many hours did YOU sleep last night? Tell us below.
stories.caption: A koala sleeps 22 hours a day. A giraffe gets by on less than 2. It's not random — it's about who's hunting whom, and how big you are. Here's the data 👇

segments.topic: average hours of sleep per day
segments.say: Sleep needs across the animal kingdom swing wildly. A koala sleeps 22 hours a day. A lion, up to 20. Humans average 8. Elephants and giraffes get by on barely 2 hours.
So why the gap? Predators like lions nap almost anywhere — 20 hours a day. Prey like giraffes can't. They get about 1.9 hours, snatched in bursts, because standing still that long gets you eaten.

segments.topic: small vs large mammal sleep hours
segments.say: Body size matters too. The tiny brown bat sleeps almost 20 hours — its fast metabolism burns out quick. A multi-ton elephant, slow metabolism, needs barely 2. Less to maintain means less recovery required.

segments.say: Right now we're near solar maximum. The sunspot number just hit 140, up from single digits back in 2019. More sunspots mean more solar storms heading our way.
NOAA ranks these storms G1 to G5. The mildest, G1, hits about 1,700 days every 11-year cycle. The most extreme, G5? Just 4 days. Rare, but not harmless.
In February 2022, one storm hit a fresh SpaceX batch mid-launch. 49 satellites went up. 82 percent of them, 40 satellites, came right back down within days.

stories.closing: Turn it down two notches. Your ears will thank you.

stories.caption: 430 million people already live with disabling hearing loss, and the WHO says that's headed past 700 million by 2050. The culprit? Everyday headphone volume blowing past the safe limit. How loud do you keep yours? 👇

segments.say: Right now, 430 million people live with disabling hearing loss. The World Health Organization expects that to pass 700 million by 2050 — one in four of us, eventually.
Here's why: a normal conversation sits around 60 decibels. The safe daily limit is 80. Max-volume headphones blast past 105, and concerts often hit 110 or higher.

segments.topic: young people in the unsafe zone
segments.say: So it's no shock that about 35 percent of people aged 12 to 34 worldwide are already stuck in that unsafe zone — over a billion of us, turning it up too loud, too often.

stories.caption: US beekeepers just lost 55.6% of their colonies in one year, the worst on record. And 60% of every managed hive in the country gets trucked to one crop every February: California almonds. Did you know that? 👇

segments.say: US beekeepers just lost 55.6 percent of their colonies in a single year, the worst die-off since records began in 2011, driven largely by mite-borne viruses.
Commercial operations, the ones renting out hives to farms, got hit even harder: 62 percent losses, compared to 51 percent for backyard beekeepers. Nobody was safe this year.
Here's why that's terrifying: about 60 percent of every managed colony in the country converges on one single crop every February, California's almond bloom. One bad year threatens the whole harvest.

In the past 20 years, elevator technology has improved significantly, with the introduction of new materials and designs, such as linear synchronous motors and advanced control systems, reducing travel time by up to 50%.

segments.say: The longest lasting dynasty, the Solomon dynasty, lasted for over 3000 years, while the shortest, the Qin dynasty, lasted only 15 years. The average length of a dynasty is around 200 years.

The top 5 longest lasting dynasties are the Solomon, Egyptian, Chinese, Roman, and British dynasties, with lengths of 3000, 2500, 2000, 1500, and 1000 years respectively. The average length of these dynasties is around 2000 years.

The length of dynasties has been declining over time, with the average length of a dynasty in the 20th century being around 50 years. The longest lasting dynasty in the 20th century was the British monarchy, which has lasted for over 1000 years.

The legacy of a dynasty can be seen in the buildings and monuments they leave behind, with some dynasties leaving a lasting impact on the world. The Egyptian pyramids, for example, are still standing after 4500 years.

stories.title: Thinnest Materials Ever Made
stories.hook: Imagine a material thinner than a human hair

segments.say: Graphene is made up of carbon atoms, with a thickness of around 0.34 nanometers, which is thinner than a human hair, with some layers measuring as low as 0.1 nanometers.
Graphene is the thinnest material, followed by silicon and aluminum, with thicknesses of 0.34 nanometers, 1.1 nanometers, and 2.5 nanometers respectively.
The market for thin materials is growing rapidly, with applications in electronics, energy, and medicine, with an expected value of 10 billion dollars by 2025, up from 1 billion dollars in 2020.

Researchers are working on developing new thin materials, with breakthroughs expected in the next 5-10 years, which could lead to significant advancements in fields like quantum computing and renewable energy.

stories.title: Probability In Everyday Life

stories.closing: Every 'lucky' moment you've ever had was just math nobody bothered to calculate.

segments.say: Flip a coin ten times in a row -- what are the odds you land ALL heads? A single flip is a coin-toss 50-50. Get exactly 5 heads in 10 flips and that's already down to 25%. Go for all 10? Under 1%.

stories.title: Saltiest Bodies of Water
stories.hook: Which lakes and seas are the saltiest on Earth?
stories.closing: The saltiest bodies of water are a wonder to behold

segments.say: The salinity levels of the world's lakes and seas vary greatly, with the Dead Sea being the saltiest, with a salinity of over 33.7% compared to the average ocean salinity of around 3.5%, and the Caspian Sea, with a salinity of around 12.8%.

The top 5 saltiest lakes in the world are the Dead Sea, with a salinity of 33.7%, Assal Lake, with a salinity of 34.8%, Lake Magadi, with a salinity of 33.2%, Don Juan Pond, with a salinity of 44.2%, and Lake Vanda, with a salinity of 35.3%.

The high salinity levels in these lakes and seas have a significant impact on the environment and the creatures that live there, with many species adapting to the high salt levels, and some even using the salt to their advantage, such as the saltwater fish that can survive in these environments.

stories.title: Stalactite Growth Rate
stories.hook: Have you ever wondered how long it takes for stalactites to form?
stories.closing: Stalactites are a testament to the power of patience in nature.

segments.say: It takes around 100 to 200 years for a stalactite to grow just one inch, with some formations taking up to 10,000 years to reach their full size.
The growth rate of stalactites is incredibly slow, with some formations growing only 0.01 inches per year, while others can grow up to 1 inch per year.

The temperature and humidity in caves remain relatively constant, with a stable temperature of around 50-60 degrees Fahrenheit and humidity levels of 80-90 percent, allowing stalactites to grow steadily over time.
Some of the most impressive stalactite formations can be found in caves around the world, with the longest formation reaching over 36 feet in length and weighing over 15 tons.
