stories.title: Moons In Our Solar System
stories.hook: Have you ever wondered how many moons are orbiting the planets in our solar system?
stories.closing: The diversity of moons in our solar system is a fascinating area of study.

segments.say: The gas giants have significantly more moons than the rocky planets, with Jupiter having 92 confirmed moons and Saturn having 83.
Ganymede, orbiting Jupiter, is the largest moon in the solar system, with a diameter of approximately 3,275 miles.
The orbital periods of moons vary significantly, ranging from less than a day for some of Jupiter's moons to over 1,000 days for Neptune's moon, Nereid.
The discovery of moons has been an ongoing process, with the first moon discovered in 1610 and many more discovered in the 20th and 21st centuries using advanced telescopes.

stories.question: Which planet do you think has the most moons and why?

stories.title: Biggest Craters In Solar System
stories.hook: The largest craters in our solar system are a testament to the violent history of our cosmos.
stories.closing: These massive craters remind us of the awe-inspiring scale and power of celestial impacts.

segments.say: The largest crater in our solar system is the Aitken Basin on the Moon, with a diameter of approximately 1,550 miles.
The formation of these massive craters occurred over billions of years, with the most significant impacts happening during the early days of the solar system.
The depth of these craters can be staggering, with some extending as deep as 2 miles below the surrounding terrain.
The exploration of these craters has been an ongoing process, with spacecraft providing valuable insights into their formation and composition.

stories.question: What do you think is the most significant factor in determining the size of a crater?

stories.title: Tree Oxygen Production
stories.hook: Did you know that trees are the ultimate oxygen factories?
stories.closing: Trees are the unsung heroes of our planet's oxygen supply.

segments.say: A single tree can produce up to 20 percent of the oxygen that one person needs in a year, with some trees producing as much as 100 pounds of oxygen per year.

While a single tree can produce a significant amount of oxygen, an entire forest can produce enough oxygen for thousands of people, making them far more effective than urban areas at producing oxygen.
Unfortunately, global tree cover has been declining over the past few decades, with some estimates suggesting that up to 15 percent of the world's tree cover has been lost since the 1990s.

However, there are many initiatives underway to plant millions of new trees and restore the world's tree cover, with some organizations aiming to plant as many as 1 trillion trees over the next few decades.

stories.question: How many trees do you think are needed to produce enough oxygen for one person?

stories.title: Poisonous Plants
stories.hook: Some plants are so toxic that they can kill you in a matter of minutes.
stories.closing: So, the next time you're out for a hike, be sure to steer clear of these deadly plants.

segments.say: The castor bean is considered one of the most poisonous plants in the world, with its seeds containing a toxin that can kill an adult human being in a matter of minutes, while tobacco plants contain a toxin that can be fatal if ingested in large quantities.
Some plants contain toxins that are so potent that they can kill an adult human being with as little as 1-2 grams of the toxin, while others may require much larger quantities to be fatal.

Unfortunately, many of these poisonous plants are found in areas where people like to hike and camp, with some estimates suggesting that up to 10 percent of all plant species in certain regions are toxic to humans.

Despite the dangers posed by these plants, researchers are still working to learn more about the toxins they contain and how they can be used to develop new medicines, with some estimates suggesting that up to 50 percent of all new medicines are derived from plant-based compounds.

stories.question: What's the most poisonous plant you've ever heard of?

    d.arc([cx - radius, cy - radius, cx + radius, cy + radius], start_ang, sweep, fill=rgba(HIGHLIGHT, 240), width=18)
    needle_ang = math.radians(sweep)
    nx = cx + radius * 0.92 * math.cos(needle_ang)
    ny = cy + radius * 0.92 * math.sin(needle_ang)
    img = images.get(labels[i]) or subject_image(labels[i])
    isz = 90
    if img is not None:
        paste(img, nx - isz / 2, ny - isz / 2, isz, isz)
    else:
        d.ellipse([nx - isz / 2, ny - isz / 2, nx + isz / 2, ny + isz / 2], fill=rgba(WARN, 230))
    text(f'{v:.0f} dB', cx, cy + radius + 30, size=42, color=TEXT, center=True)
    text(labels[i].upper(), cx, cy + radius + 78, size=22, color=TEXT, center=True)

stories.title: Heaviest Things Ever Moved
stories.hook: Imagine shifting a mountain, literally!
stories.closing: The power of human ingenuity is mind-boggling!

segments.say: The ancient Egyptians moved blocks weighing up to 2.5 tons to build the pyramids, while the Romans relocated a 1,000-ton statue of Apollo.
Today, we can move massive oil rigs weighing over 100,000 tons and ships that displace more than 200,000 tons of water.
Throughout history, the heaviest lifts include the 6,000-ton USS Illinois, the 12,000-ton London Bridge, and the 50,000-ton Yucca Mountain nuclear waste repository tunnel boring machine.

These massive moves are made possible by advanced engineering, with cranes capable of lifting up to 20% of their own weight, and specialized transporters that can handle up to 50% of the cargo's weight.

stories.question: What's the heaviest thing you think could be moved?

stories.title: Pyramid Construction Timeline
stories.hook: The ancient pyramids were built in a remarkably short time!
stories.closing: The ingenuity and labor that went into these structures are a testament to human determination!

segments.say: The Great Pyramid of Giza, the largest of the three, is believed to have taken around 20 to 30 years to construct, with a workforce of around 100,000 laborers.
The Pyramid of Khafre, the second-largest pyramid, is thought to have taken around 15 to 20 years to build, while the Pyramid of Menkaure, the smallest of the three, took around 10 to 15 years.
It's estimated that the labor force for the Great Pyramid of Giza was around 100,000 workers, which is roughly 10% of the Egyptian population at the time.
The construction speed of the pyramids is estimated to be around 10 blocks per day, with the Great Pyramid of Giza requiring around 2.3 million blocks to complete.

stories.question: How do you think the pyramids could have been built so quickly?

stories.title: Oldest Written Languages
stories.hook: What's the oldest language still spoken today?
stories.closing: The history of language is a story of human connection.

segments.say: The oldest written language, Sumerian, is over 4,500 years old, while Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs are around 3,300 years old.
As civilizations rose and fell, languages spread across the globe, with Latin influencing many European languages.
Some languages, like Chinese, have been continuously written for over 3,000 years, while others, like Ancient Greek, have had a resurgence in popularity.
The evolution of language is a continuous process, with new languages emerging and old ones adapting to the changing world, like the rise of pidgins and creoles.

stories.question: What's your favorite ancient language?

stories.title: Speed of News
stories.hook: How fast did news travel in the past?
stories.closing: The speed of news has come a long way.

segments.say: In the past, news traveled at the speed of horses, taking around 10 days to cross the United States, while trains reduced this time to just 3 days.
The invention of the telegraph revolutionized news travel, allowing messages to be sent across the country in just 10 minutes.
Today, news travels at the speed of light, with information being transmitted around the world in just a few seconds.
Social media has further accelerated the spread of news, with information going viral in a matter of minutes.

stories.question: What's the fastest way you've ever received news?

stories.title: Largest Numbers Named
stories.hook: You won't believe the names behind the biggest numbers in maths
stories.closing: The named numbers that shape our understanding of scale

segments.say: The largest named numbers include Graham's number and googolplex, with values that are almost unimaginable, like 10 to the power of 100 and a 1 followed by a googol of zeros.
To put these numbers into perspective, consider that a googol is a 1 followed by 100 zeros, and a googolplex is a 1 followed by a googol of zeros, making them almost incomprehensibly large.
These enormous numbers have practical applications in mathematics, such as combinatorics and number theory, helping us understand complex problems and patterns.

The study and naming of large numbers have evolved over time, reflecting advancements in mathematical understanding and the need for new terms to describe the vastness of the universe and its phenomena.

stories.question: What's the largest named number you've ever come across?

stories.title: Language Disappearance Rate
stories.hook: Languages are disappearing at an alarming rate, but what's behind this trend?
stories.closing: The urgent need to preserve linguistic diversity

segments.say: It's estimated that a language goes extinct every 14 days, with over 40% of languages at risk, highlighting the urgent need for preservation efforts.
The majority of languages are spoken by small communities, with over 50% of languages having fewer than 10,000 speakers, making them vulnerable to extinction.
Globalization, urbanization, and lack of documentation are major factors contributing to language extinction, as younger generations shift towards dominant languages.
Efforts to document and preserve languages are underway, including language education programs, linguistic research, and community engagement, offering hope for the future of linguistic diversity.

stories.question: What can be done to prevent language extinction?

stories.closing: At the Arabian Plate's rate, the Red Sea will be a brand new ocean before it's done growing.

segments.say: The Pacific Plate is creeping 3 centimeters every year, the North American Plate 2 -- invisible, but it never stops.

stories.title: Longest Rivers On Earth
stories.hook: Have you ever wondered where the longest rivers in the world are located?
stories.closing: The world's longest rivers support a vast array of ecosystems and human populations.

segments.say: The Nile, Amazon, and Yangtze rivers are the longest, with lengths of 6,695, 6,400, and 6,300 kilometers respectively.
The Amazon River basin covers an area of approximately 7 million square kilometers, while the Nile River basin covers around 3.4 million square kilometers.
The Amazon River has the highest discharge volume, with an average of 209,000 cubic meters per second, followed by the Congo River with 48,000 cubic meters per second.
Rivers support around 10% of all fish species, with the Amazon River alone being home to over 2,000 species.

stories.question: What's your favorite river and why?

stories.title: Creepy Creatures Never Stop Growing
stories.hook: What if some animals just keep getting bigger and bigger?
stories.closing: These creatures will keep on growing, and it's mind-blowing!

segments.say: Some fish can grow up to 10 times their original size, while others can increase their weight by 50 times, like the gigantic whale shark, which can weigh over 20 tons.
The growth rate of these creatures can be staggering, with some increasing their size by 50% in just a few months, like the python, which can grow up to 10 feet in a year.
The largest species of fish, like the whale shark and the basking shark, can grow up to 40 feet in length, while the largest squid can weigh over 1,000 pounds.

While some creatures can grow indefinitely, others have a growth limit, like the green anole lizard, which can grow up to 5 inches in length, but no more, and the axolotl, which can regrow its limbs, but only up to a certain point.

stories.title: Sharp Teeth In The Wild
stories.hook: What's the sharpest tooth in the animal kingdom?
stories.closing: These sharp teeth will leave you in awe!

segments.say: The great white shark has the sharpest teeth in the ocean, with some teeth reaching a sharpness of 100 times that of human teeth, and the saltwater crocodile has the sharpest teeth on land, with a bite force of over 3,700 pounds per square inch.
The length of the teeth can also be impressive, with the walrus having tusks that can reach up to 3 feet in length, and the narwhal having a tusk that can reach up to 10 feet in length.

Some animals, like sharks and snakes, can replace their teeth throughout their lifetime, with some sharks going through thousands of teeth in a single year, and the snake shedding its skin, including its teeth, every few weeks.

The function of the teeth can also be fascinating, with some animals using their teeth for defense, like the porcupine, which has quills that can deter predators, and the armadillo, which has a shell that can protect it from predators.

stories.question: What's the sharpest tooth you've ever seen in person?
